data.title: VNQ: Volatility Makes Commercial Real Estate A Buy
data.description:
The theme of the day at REITer’s Digest is investor psychology. Today, we will dive into a discussion about REITs, real estate, and the fundamentals of investing, highlighting thoughts from investors who are far smarter than yours truly. In the context of an increasingly volatile environment, we will begin to assemble the pieces of the discussion to create a more comprehensive forward strategy.

As real estate, and the rest of the market, experiences an unprecedented level of volatility, there may be opportunities for investors with a long-time horizon.

Throughout this article, we will focus on the Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ), which is one of the largest index funds investing in the commercial real estate sector. VNQ is a market capitalization weighted index fund that invests in some of the largest publicly traded real estate investment trusts in the world. In this article, we will provide an overview of the fund including performance, holdings, and the expenses. In the broader context of commercial real estate, we will discuss how VNQ has become victim to investor psychology and the whipsaw effect of repricing. While commercial real estate remains a low volatility, stable asset class, the share price of REITs around the world continues to bounce around with the ongoing announcement of novel trade policy.

ETF Overview

My prior coverage of VNQ was in February. Over this time, the fund itself has not changed significantly, but the market has. Those looking for more detail on the fund’s construction should refer to the prior coverage.

VNQ is still the largest ETF in the real estate sector. As usual, it feels prudent to explain that I use the term real estate sector because the fund includes investments in brokerage houses and advisors instead of real estate investment trusts exclusively. Historically, the main point that I have highlighted with VNQ is that the fund invests far and wide targeting both the good and the bad.

The fund is cost-efficient charging just 13 basis points of assets under management and is backed by one of the world's largest asset managers. The fund is liquid with tens of millions of shares trading daily and is even one of the few REIT exchange-traded funds to have options. By all accounts, those who are looking for a real estate ETF should consider VNQ.

That's my prior coverage of the fund, share prices have fallen by nearly 10%. Falling from more than $90 per share to just over $80 per share in two months has highlighted the extreme volatility in the marketplace.

So, let’s discuss.

Investor Psychology & Real Estate

As highlighted, the past several months have been a volatile time for the commercial real estate industry. While commercial real estate has historically been a low volatility, cash flow backed asset class, the modern era of pandemics, rising interest rates, and trade disputes has created an increasingly uncertain environment which has begun to shake a historically dependable asset class.

In fact, uncertainty has become so great around the commercial real estate sector that words and phrases like “freezing”, “locked up”, and “collapsing” have already come into play. Just today, Jared Toothman, the New York market leader for Lincoln Property Company, discussed paralysis within the commercial real estate sector.

People can't think past two weeks,” Lincoln Property Co. NYC Market Leader Jared Toothman said this morning at Bisnow’s New York Finance event.

But CRE has gotten awfully accustomed to uncertainty. Stubborn inflation, the future of the office, where valuations will reset, when interest rates will come down — those problems have had the industry holding a cautious stance for years.

At some point, CRE has to step away from macroeconomics and decide whether projects can stand on their own fundamentals. It’s a long, long game, after all.

“If you've got a partner who says a [deal] is pausing because of a week of Trump trading, then you probably don't want to be with that partner on a development deal for the next five years,” Toothman said. “So, it's good to see who's serious and who's not right now.

Toothman highlights, there is an extraordinary amount of weight placed on short-term thinking when it comes to making long-term decisions. In the development game, participants are forced to think months if not years ahead to gauge what the market will be willing to absorb and at what price it will purchase it. Like other periods of volatility such as the pandemic, capital partners within the development market have become increasingly unsteady and unwilling to deploy capital.

For participants in the commercial real estate industry, this means a significant slowdown in velocity and a reduced supply that will be anticipated to come online in coming years. With the priority placed on velocity in the commercial real estate market, slowing has little benefits for investors, developers, or even the people living in the United States.

Let us take a moment to step back and approach commercial real estate from a different angle. From my perspective, commercial real estate remains one of the most stable investments from a long-term perspective. Aside from the ebbs and flows of specific asset classes, I believe the merits of real estate will remain fundamentally unchanged over the coming decades. Without any real change to this thesis, observing participants begin to act somewhat irrationally makes me believe many need to hit the brakes.

Let’s shift gears and highlight a thought from Howard Marks on the world of private credit. Howard Marks became a legend in the bond industry through leadership in private lending. This means credit investing in debt that is illiquid or has no secondary market, such as corporate bonds and treasuries. This means that these bonds have no daily liquidity or mark to market, like commercial real estate. Marks has historically highlighted this lack of marking to market in his insights on private credit, such as Gimme Credit.

What about the lack of marking to market, and the resulting low level of volatility? It’s obviously unrealistic to think the value of private loans doesn’t fluctuate. But on the other hand, it seems many people consider the non-marking to market a plus, in that they can report that their investments didn’t go down much in a difficult environment. Private credit managers are supposed to mark their holdings to reality based on fundamentals, but that’s clearly less volatile (and less objective) than marking to a market. On the other hand, is it desirable that public asset prices reflect every up and down of investor psychology? Not marking to market may be unrealistic, but it may be welcome. (Investors in public securities could have the same experience if they refused to read the newspapers and tossed their brokerage statements in the drawer, but such behavior would be called irresponsible.)

The conclusion that Marks lays out could be one of the most important pieces of wisdom in a highly volatile market. Investors in public securities could perhaps save themselves the insanity of watching today's whipsaw market by simply ignoring daily pricing of REITs and focusing on the fundamental merits of the assets or REITs themselves through changes in quarterly cash flow. It is hard to believe that the asset values of many pieces of commercial real estate have declined as precipitously as VNQ’s share price over the past month.

However, herein lies a critical distinction between REITs and commercial real estate itself.

REITs vs. Real Estate

The world of REITs and commercial real estate has several critical differentiators. Foremost, the similarities are obvious. Typically, REITs hold commercial real estate, making them a scalable and efficient opportunity for the retail investor to access high-quality commercial real estate that costs millions of dollars. For many of us, it may be our only option to invest in these assets. However, that does not mean that investing in each has identical consequences.

Leaving aside the world of non-traded REITs which are valued at their net asset value as calculated by the fund and verified by auditors, the world of publicly traded REITs has little connection to the asset values.

This concept was described well by Chris Volk with Seeking Alpha Analyst Brad Thomas.

REIT investors are not buying into mutual funds of real estate. They're buying into real estate operating companies. The former presumes that returns will be derived from the buying and selling of real estate. The latter presumes that investor returns will be derived from corporate business model operating fundamentals.

NAV does have one benefit. Investors can estimate the break-up value of the real estate operating companies in which they invest. This is certainly a tried-and-true approach taken by activist shareholders. But remember to keep in mind that estimating asset values is only part of the game. You also must estimate the costs to eliminate or assume the liability stack.

Whereas public REIT values reflect the worth of their operating business models, non-traded REITs have traditionally issued and repurchased shares based on their reported NAVs. Of course, they're operating real estate companies, too, making this practice wholly inconsistent with their investment fundamentals.

Investors buying public REITs should have several key takeaways from this. First and foremost, publicly traded REITs are not traded with a relationship with their net asset value. Instead, they are priced by the operating performance of each share, which can be expressed through a variety of metrics at different levels of the business such as adjusted funds from operations per share, cash available for distribution per share, or even more unique metrics like net debt to EBITDA.

In a period of uncertainty or a recession, the operating performance of publicly traded REITs is likely to become more volatile than the transactional prices of the assets themselves. This has led to the extraordinary decline of REITs like Rexford Industrial (REXR) and Americold Realty (COLD), both of which are exposed to trade policies.

While their real estate remains top-notch from a long-term investment perspective, both companies have fallen sharply as investors anticipate the impact on the bottom line in coming quarters. I believe this combines with other investor behavior to generate the increasing volatility of public real estate.

Public REITs are more liquid than private REITs and far more liquid than commercial real estate. By this, I mean that selling shares of VNQ is a quicker process than submitting a redemption request in a private REIT with quarterly liquidity, such as BREIT. Selling these shares is also much faster, and considerably less expensive, than starting the disposition process for a commercial asset worth millions of dollars. This means that funds will generally look to their stack of public REITs first when making thematic decisions.

Right now, I would venture to guess many investors are looking to cut their exposure to real estate, which is typically considered a risk forward asset.

In a world where REITs offer a modest yield over the ten-year treasury yield, making the decision to reduce risk looks attractive with safe alternatives that offer a real return. This is yet again a reflection of Marks’ point that the pricing of liquid assets is a reflection of daily thinking around investments. When paired with commercial real estate which is relatively illiquid, it means that REIT pricing has a better chance of being misaligned with long-term fundamentals.

Conclusion

From my chair, volatility breeds opportunity in commercial real estate. While investors make decisions based on current events, the fundamentals of Class A commercial real estate remain unchanged over the course of decades. If we are willing to wait decades for an investment to pan out, buying in a down market when pricing is well-off of all-time highs could be a prudent decision. VNQ earns a Buy rating amidst a volatile real estate market.

data.title: UPS: Too Cheap To Ignore (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Intro

United Parcel Service, Inc. (UPS) stock has been bruised in the past year, with losses totaling over 35%. While technical weakness may continue for the stock, the valuation has become too cheap to ignore. In the below analysis, I have determined that most technicals remain highly bearish with the moving averages and the Ichimoku Clouds showing an undesirable outlook. There are some near term positive signs in the indicators and there was a bullish engulfing pattern recently. However, with the lack of other bottoming signals, further declines may occur. For the fundamentals, Q4 results were mixed and full year 2025 guidance was on the weak side for sure. However, the P/S ratio's contraction to multiyear lows seems to be overdone with revenue growth expected to remain at around average levels. The stock seems significantly undervalued, especially with its major discount versus the Industrials sector. While further technical weakness is likely from here, the stock seems too cheap to ignore currently, and so I believe a buy rating is warranted at this juncture.

Technical Analysis

The Basics

Trends, Support & Resistance

UPS is currently in a near term downtrend. This trend line began back in late January and last provided resistance in late March. However, the formation of a bullish engulfing pattern on the previous trading day could signal that there's a reversal for the stock. There are three additional resistance levels other than the downtrend line. The first is at around 109. This level was a downside gap early this month that occurred on the highest volume of the month thus far. This level was also supported back in late January and early February. The next resistance level is at around 122. This area was strongly supported back in December while being key resistance in early March. Lastly, there is distant resistance at about 134. The major gap down in January occurred at this level on by far the highest volume in the past six months. While there are no major support levels for UPS on this chart, the bullish engulfing pattern could act as some support, as a break below it would void the reversal signal.

Moving Averages & Indicators

The 50-day SMA has been below the 200-day SMA for the entirety of the past six months to show that long-term sentiment has been consistently bearish. The gap between the SMAs has continued to widen since late January and so bearish momentum is still increasing for the long term currently. For the EMAs, the latest crossover was a bearish one that occurred back in late January. The 9 day EMA failed to break above in March and instead bounced off the 21 day EMA as a sign of resilient bears. Currently, the gap between the EMAs is significant and so near term momentum is still bearish.

The stock gapped below the Bollinger Band's midline back in late January to show that a downtrend may be in its early stages. UPS was able to break back above for a brief period in early March, but ultimately, the stock slumped back below to show that a downtrend was indeed occurring. Recently, the stock has dipped below the lower band to show an oversold state, but the bounce on the previous trading day has moved the stock back within the bands. For the MACD, there was a bearish crossover in late March. The deviation between the lines grew substantially to reflect the major increase in bearish sentiment, but on the previous and current trading days, the histogram has turned bullish to show that near term sentiment has likely bottomed. However, the MACD still sits at six month lows, confirming the recent plunge in the stock. For RSI, it has rebounded from an extremely bearish reading of below 30 to a level of 31.97 currently. This shows a slight loosening of the bears' grasp but, nonetheless, they remain in solid control. Like the MACD, the RSI confirms the stock's declines with its own long-term low set just days ago. Lastly, for the stochastics, there was a true bullish crossover recently as the %K was able to break above the %D within the 20 zone. Furthermore, the %K seems to be breaking above 20 now as another sign that the bears are retreating at least for the near term.

Takeaway

Overall, I would say that the technical outlook, presented in these classic analysis tools, remains bearish. The stock remains in a downtrend, the moving averages demonstrate bearish momentum, while the MACD and RSI confirm the plunge in the stock. There are some positive near term signs, however, like the MACD histogram turning positive and the stochastics having a bullish crossover that show there are some near term reprieve for UPS. The bullish engulfing pattern could be a significant reversal signal for the long term, but with the lack of other bottoming signals, its reliability may be questioned until further notice.

Fibonacci

Retracement

Using the late October high as the starting point and the previous day's low as the end point, some potential price targets or resistance levels are shown above. The nearest level is 23.6% which is at around 104 for UBS. The next level could be an even more significant one. The 36.2% level sits just above 110, a round number. Furthermore, as identified in the charting analysis above, there is a resistance level quite near 110 as well. Therefore, that area could be major resistance for the stock if it's able to stage a major bounce.

Projection

Using the late October high again, the January low, and the retracement to just above 120, some support levels are show by the Fibonacci Projection in this chart. The stock broke beneath the 61.8% level on the third trading day this month and has since experienced resistance there due to the reversal in polarity. Due to the fact that the 61.8% level coincides with the 100 price level, this area's importance is amplified. The next level of support for UBS is the 100% level which is in the mid to high 80s price range and so the stock could have further to fall.

Ichimoku Cloud (Advanced)

The stock has remained consistently below the cloud since the start of December and currently, it sits far below as a sign of strong general bearishness. The cloud itself has been bearish for the vast majority of the past six months. The cloud is not overly thick as of now, but moving in to May, with the deviation between the Leading Span A and Leading Span B having increased significantly, there could be heavy resistance for the stock in the near future. For near term momentum, the Conversion Line spent a brief period above the baseline in late March, but ultimately, there was a bearish crossover. Currently, the gap between these lines continues to expand to show that near term bearish momentum is still increasing. Lastly, despite the stock's bounce on the previous trading day, the Lagging Span remains far below UPS' levels in late February, confirming the downtrend. Looking back, there was a key sell signal back in late March. The cloud itself was bearish, the stock was well beneath the cloud, the Conversion Line and baseline had a bearish crossover, and the Lagging Span broke beneath the early February stock price. This was a sign of things to come for UPS stock that should not have been ignored.

Fundamental Analysis

Earnings

Quarterly Results

At the end of January, UPS released their earnings for 2024 Q4 and showed mixed results. They reported consolidated revenues of $25.3 billion, up from $24.9 billion in the prior year period. Their U.S. Domestic Segment and International Segment both saw YoY gains in revenue but Supply Chain Solutions weighed on results with its revenue decline. The decrease in revenue was attributed to the divestiture of Coyote. For EPS, the company reported an adjusted figure of $2.75, up 11.3% YoY. UPS had a mixed performance versus analyst expectations as revenues missed by $114.12 million while EPS beat by $0.22. As shown above, revenue growth saw QoQ declines and shows that the resurgence in the figure has stalled for the time being. Still, current levels of revenue growth are at above average levels compared to the past few years even though it's only near zero. Lastly, as you can see above, the operating margin of the business has shown both YoY and QoQ improvement. Operating margin is now at its highest level since 2023 Q2 and so the efficiency of the business seems to be on the right track. Overall, I would say these earnings results were a bit mixed. The financials as a whole seem resilient, but there is no question that growth has been underwhelming and remains at sluggish levels.

Guidance

UPS did not provide guidance specific to their 2025 Q1, but they did provide a brief full-year outlook. For the full year 2025, they are expected to see revenues of approximately $89.0 billion. For 2024, they had total revenues of $91.1 billion and so implied growth from this guidance is -2.3%. In 2023, revenues were reported to be $91.0 billion and so for 2024 they were able to generate growth of +0.11%, still better than the negative growth projected for this year. Overall, I believe it is clear that UPS is expecting a soft 2025 as the growth recovery is continuing to stall. Furthermore, Seeking Alpha currently has a C- Revision Grade for the company as 96.55% of FY1 Revisions have been downward in the past 90 days, showing that analysts expect weakness in their earnings moving forward.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio [P/S]

Despite the company's struggles as discussed above, I believe the stock is significantly undervalued at current levels. The P/S ratio has been on a multiyear downtrend and major contraction has occurred so far in 2025. The multiple is now at rock bottom levels, very near the three-year low set just days ago. As discussed above, revenue growth has remained above average despite the QoQ declines. In addition, while 2025 full-year guidance shows that revenue growth will dip back into negative territory, a -2.3% growth rate is still above the multiyear average, as you can see in the revenue growth chart above. While the outlook is definitely weaker than desired, the P/S ratio's contraction seems to be overdone and is an overreaction to the modestly worsening outlook for the company, Currently, the Industrials sector has a median P/S ratio of 1.36 and so UPS is at a 33.4% discount to peers. While the fundamentals are sluggish as of now, with the company being a key transport provider worldwide, I believe this discount is overly harsh. Seeking Alpha currently holds an A- Valuation Grade for UPS, confirming my evaluation.

Conclusion

In the above analysis, it was determined that most technicals remain bearish for UPS stock. There are some near term positive signs in the indicators but the bullish engulfing pattern is unable to convince me that the long-term bottom is in. For the fundamentals, most recent earnings were lackluster and full year 2025 guidance was overall soft. However, the P/S ratio's contraction shows that investors have overreacted to the weakening fundamentals, as revenue growth is actually projected to remain at around average levels relative to the past few years. Therefore, I believe the stock has become a buy with the valuation being too cheap to ignore. That said, further technical losses are possible and investors should keep that in mind.
